cards.text: A destructor has the same name as the class, preceded by a {{c1::tilde (~)}} symbol, and takes {{c2::no parameters}}.
A destructor is automatically called when an object goes {{c1::out of scope}} or is explicitly {{c2::deleted}}.
Destructors are primarily used to {{c1::deallocate memory}} or {{c2::release resources}} acquired by the object.
The RAII idiom stands for {{c1::Resource Acquisition Is Initialization}}, where resources are acquired in the {{c2::constructor}} and released in the {{c3::destructor}}.
A class can only have {{c1::one}} destructor, and destructors cannot be {{c2::overloaded}}.

cards.front: What happens if you don't define a destructor for a class?
cards.back: The compiler will generate a default destructor that does nothing except call destructors for all member objects.

cards.front: In what order are destructors called for class members?
cards.back: In the reverse order of construction: last constructed, first destructed.

cards.question: Write a destructor for a class named Database that closes a connection.
cards.explanation: Destructor has same name as class with ~ prefix, no return type, no parameters.

cards.question: In what order will destructors be called?
cards.explanation: Objects are destructed in reverse order of construction: c is destroyed first, then b, then a.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Memory leak! The dynamically allocated array is never deleted.
cards.explanation: Should add destructor: ~Array() { delete[] m_data; } to prevent memory leak.

RAII is a C++ programming idiom where:
  • Resources (memory, files, locks, etc.) are acquired in the constructor
  • Resources are automatically released in the destructor
  • Ensures resources are always cleaned up, even with exceptions
  • Makes resource management automatic and exception-safe

cards.back: Special member function called automatically when an object is destroyed, used to clean up resources (syntax: ~ClassName())
